sentences.text: The seventh experiment was seawater, conducted to develop a method of making seawater potable-- or drinkable.

The victims were German, Czech, and Polish gypsies-- this was a gypsy experiment-- who were deprived of food and given only brackish yellow seawater for weeks, which resulted in excruciating pain, foaming at the mouth, and eventually, in most cases, madness.
One of the most dramatic courtroom scenes occurred during my turn in the courtroom.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Karl Hoellenrainer, H-O-E-L-L-E-N-R-A-I-N-E-R, was a dark haired man of small stature who was nervous and very excitable as he assumed the witness stand in the courtroom.
The main courtroom of the Palace of Justice looked very large that day in front of this little man.
It was June 27, 1947.
Question-- now witness, for what reasons were you arrested by the Gestapo on 29 May, 1944?
The question was asked in English, and interpreted through the English Channel of my earphones into German.
The answer given in German was, because I am a gypsy of mixed blood.
The Reich Citizenship Law, dated April 25, 1943, read, quote, 'Jews and gypsies cannot become citizens,' unquote.

The Law for the Protection of German Blood and German Honor, dated November 14, 1935, which prohibited marriage between gypsies and Germans read, quote, 'a marriage shall furthermore not be contracted if the progeny to be expected from it would endanger the purity of German blood,' unquote.
Karl Hoellenrainer was first sent to Auschwitz, then Buchenwald, and then Dachau, where he underwent these seawater experiments, which tortured his body and mind for weeks.
Question-- do you think you would be able to recognize that doctor if you saw him today?
Answer-- yes, immediately.
I would recognize him at once.
Question-- would you kindly stand up from your witness chair, take your earphones off, and proceed over to the defendant's dock and see if you can recognize the professor that you met at Dachau?
Everyone in the courtroom waited tensely, just watching him.
He stood up and then paused for just a moment with his eyes seemingly fixed on a doctor in the second row of the prisoner's dock.
And then in a flash he was gone from the witness stand.
He just seemed to be almost leaping over the ends of the German defense counsel's tables toward the prisoner's dock.
Just almost appeared to be flying, with his right arm stretched high, reaching for Dr.
Beiglboeck, the consulting physician to the German Air Force, the Luftwaffe.
And there was instant-- we were all in kind of a state of shock.
There was confusion immediately in the courtroom.

And three of our American MPs who were standing behind the prisoner's dock rushed up in front and grabbed this little man almost in mid-air, who was just futilely bent on delivering his own brand of justice.
And they subdued him just short of his target.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Moments later he stood before our distinguished chief judge of the four judge panel, Walter [?
Biells ?] of Seattle, who sentenced him to 90 days in the Nuremberg prison for contempt of court.

Which was certainly kind of a shock to me, because I had already taken the testimony of all of this torture that he had undergone, to know that he was going to have to be sent to jail at that period-- for that period of time.
So it took all of us a little bit of time to recover from that just momentarily.
And it really made me think very hard about the decorum that has to be kept in the courtroom, and what the judge had to do.
I do not know, and I never knew, whether he really had to serve the entire 90 days.
But-- and I didn't see a weapon.
But later I learned from an American military police officer that he actually had a dagger in his hand.
So after that period of time, then security on entering the courtroom, by witnesses or the public, was greatly tightened, because witnesses would not have had a top security pass.
They would have had admittance by request from an attorney for their testimony.
And I don't know the details of that kind of security that was required for them.
Then the next experiment was epidemic jaundice, conducted from 1943 to '45 at Sachsenhausen and Natzweiler.
And these were Polish Jews who were deliberately infected with epidemic jaundice, resulting in many deaths.

The ninth experiment was sterilization, conducted from 1941 to 1945-- one of the longest periods of time for experiments-- at Auschwitz and Ravensbrueck on Russian prisoners of war and Polish Jews, to develop a suitable method for the sterilization of large population growth groups by radiation, drugs, and surgery, in a planned mass genocide.
This resulted in horrible physical and mental pain and anguish.
And this was one of the experiments that was brought out in the movie, with Spencer Tracy.
The tenth experiment was typhus, conducted from 1941 to '45-- a long period of time-- at Buchenwald and Natzweiler to determine the effectiveness of vaccines.
These victims became raving mad, suffered delirium, and 90% died a slow and frightful death.
These were not only German criminals taken from their prisons, but Poles, Russians, and Frenchmen as well.
The purpose was to manufacture a typhus convalescent serum.

And infection was accomplished by laceration of the skin, and the introduction of a typhus virus by contagious lice or by the intravenous or intramuscular injection of fresh blood containing the typhus virus.
Now to maintain a constant source of fresh infected blood, three to five inmates per month were artificially infected to provide the blood when it was needed.
And substantially, all of these people died.
And in Denver we have a survivor who told me that of 50 people in his block who were selected for this experiment, 17 survived.
And he was one of them.
The 11th experiment was poison, conducted from 1943 to '44 at Buchenwald and Sachsenhausen to investigate the effect of poison on humans.
Now some of these 12 experiments had absolutely no scientific objective to heal or cure, but rather to kill or exterminate.
And this was one of them.
Poison bullets were shot into Russian prisoners of war, not to test an antidote for poison but to determine how fast death occurred, taking up to two hours and nine minutes.
Poison was also secretly administered in food.
And the doctors stood behind curtains to watch the reactions.
All died, or were deliberately killed, to permit autopsies.
The photographs and anthropological measurements were taken of these selected victims.
And then they were killed and defleshed.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Military Tribunal One was established on October 25, 1946, to hear this medical case, concluded in July 1947.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Seven doctors, including Karl Brandt, were sentenced to death by hanging, five sentenced to life imprisonment, two sentenced to 20 years-- one to 15 years and one to 10 years-- and seven were found not guilty and released.
And somehow that was another shock to me, to have so many found not guilty, or acquitted and released.
I felt maybe there were several who might have been.

Now when the defendants-- and I'll never forget this scene-- who were sentenced to death by hanging, each one, including Karl Brandt, stood before the four judge tribunal to hear this sentence, flanked by American MP-- MPs-- military police.
Each one threw the Heil Hitler salute at the judges, clicked the heels of his boots, and turned and arrogantly strode out of that courtroom under guard with his head held high.
There was not a sign of any kind of remorse shown.
But after we had heard the testimony and their rationalizations, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the case of Karl Brandt, his-- he knew he was a doctor, that he had taken the Hippocratic oath.
But he felt that order and obedience took priority.
He had received his orders, and it was obedience to those orders.
The charter that was set up took the [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] possibility of their claiming innocence by virtue of following orders into consideration.

And one of the rules in the-- the charter, or the subsequent rules that were set up governing these trials, was that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there could not be a legitimate defense to the following of orders.
--that we reported.
I understood that-- I had the feeling, because I had also talked to judges out of the courtroom.
I knew that there was a feeling on the part of the judges that they wanted to be very careful in trying these cases not to have the world view, these as predetermined trials of revenge, of vengeance.
And I had the feeling that the judges tried very hard to be especially fair for this reason, and I felt, even bend over backwards in their judgments and in their sentences.
I do not remember distinctly the particular charges against the doctors who were acquitted.

But I felt that it was because they may not have had direct-- direct participation in these experiments, but may have been kind of in positions where they were ordered to do minor preparatory work on the victims.
I really don't know, Gary.
But that was-- as we court reporters did not really know what went on in their-- the judges' deliberations-- any more than we know in a multi-judge tribunal today, or the Supreme Court, for example.
Yes.
We made-- I made eye contact.
I mean, occasionally they would look at us.
And especially-- especially Karl Brandt.
I would not look at him as I walked by him, because I had seen him leering at another woman reporter as she walked by him, a very attractive blond woman who was older.
You know, we walked by them so close.
We were so close to them.
I mean closely walking by them.
And I just made a point of not, as I walked by him, and he was right on that corner, within arm's reach as I walked by, after I saw the way he looked at the other-- the one woman.

But sitting across from them listening, it's the same feeling that I had years and years later, all through the years, as I-- as an official court reporter in the Denver district court system here in murder trials.
Listen to the testimony, and then would look at the defendant on just a pause.
Just look at the defendant, to study his face-- especially eyes-- you know, to see what I could read.
In the case of these doctors, these were the Germans.
And their medical system, their medicine, and their research, and what they had achieved in medicine, was probably the highest, at the highest level in the world at that time.
And they-- I-- I-- it was so difficult for me to even understand.
You know, when we're born with a free will, and we know right from wrong.

And just abdicate that totally for the Fatherland-- every one of them used that term-- to build a pure-blooded German race-- this was their goal, the extermination of all people who were not too-- who were not eligible, not qualified, to be part of this super race.
That ran as a primary [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] goal through their lives and in the work that they did.
There wasn't any doubt about it.
Well I had, of course, the greatest, greatest sympathy, as we did.
I mean it was almost like an empathetic kind of suffering, just from hearing their testimony.
It was so hard not to-- I mean to write.
And whenever I thought I was going to be going into tears at what I was hearing, I would keep my head down, you know, as I was writing.
It was such a horrifying experience for us.
Now you know, I was pretty young, 22, 21 and 22.
And most of the reporters there were in their 30s and 40s.
Some were 45.

And they had spent years in courtrooms, wherever they worked, listening to maybe some pretty horrifying testimony-- on, you know, serial murders, or horrible murders, for example-- that I had not been subject to.
By that time I may have reported a, you know, outside of police headquarters.
I may have reported courtroom cases of murders maybe only half a dozen times before I went to Nuremberg.
So to me it really affected me very badly.
The whole-- the whole period of time there affected me for my entire life.
I never recovered from Nuremberg.
I never recovered from what I heard there.
And I came back with a different outlook on life.

And you know, I could-- when I got to New York, for example, back to the big city with all the cocktail parties, and all the materialism and all-- I just-- for all of what I had heard, I really wanted to just turn around and go back to Europe, for some strange reason.

Well I guess that having grown up in a small town, Woodstock, with one Jewish family, I did not know very much about bigotry, and especially on a scale of calculated genocide that the German hierarchy was involved in to exterminate all of the Jews of Europe-- not all of the Christians of Europe, as I have said.
So I came back home with a whole new feeling, especially for Jews, and interest in.

And when I got back to-- when my husband and I eventually got back to Detroit-- and I married an American MP officer over there-- I-- I had the feeling that I wanted to know more than I even knew then.
And I can remember there was an ad in for-- about a videotape from the war trials at a Jewish synagogue.
I didn't know what a Jewish synagogue was, other than that was the place of worship for the Jews as the church was for me.
And I-- I went to this synagogue alone at night to see this film.
I have never seen that film anywhere since.
But it was of the ovens.
And I just remember that the mothers being sent to the ovens with the babies.
Now this may have been-- I don't know whether this was part of Mengele's work at Auschwitz, or what the origin of the film was, but it was film from Nazi Germany.
And I remember the mothers alive being strapped to the oven cots that they slid in and out, and the babies being strapped to them, so that the live baby was over the live mother, strapped.
And that the person in charge of the ovens, in this case, opened the oven door, and flames shot out.
And they pushed the cot with the living mother and baby into the flames alive and slammed the door shut.
And after that, you know, I mean I was just continually with [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some terrible kind of suffering.
So it was all part of this horror story for me to eventually start speaking on this subject.
And it seemed as though all the years after that I was busy raising two children, traveling with my military officer husband to various bases in the United States, you know.
But eventually became so active in speaking.
End of tape five.
